Unit 22: Advanced Particles & Spoken Expressions

To sound like a native, you need to master the tiny, untranslatable words that Greeks sprinkle into their speech. These "conversational particles" add flavor, doubt, sarcasm, or intense emotion to any sentence.

1. Curiosity & Doubt

Άραγε (I wonder...)

Placed at the beginning or end of a question. - Άραγε θα έρθει η Μαρία; (I wonder if Maria will come?) - Τι σκέφτεται άραγε; (What is he thinking, I wonder?)

Τάχα & Δήθεν (Supposedly / "Yeah, right")

Used to show extreme sarcasm or disbelief. - Κλαίει δήθεν επειδή χτύπησε. (He is supposedly crying [but I know he's faking]). - Ναι, τάχα μου! (Yeah, right! / Allegedly!)

2. Wishes & Hopes

Μακάρι (If only / Let's hope)

Triggers the Subjunctive (να) or stands alone as a hopeful response. - Μακάρι να περάσω τις εξετάσεις! (I hope I pass!) - Person A: Θα βρούμε τραπέζι; ➔ Person B: Μακάρι! (Let's hope!)

3. Direction & Commands

Ας (Let's / Let it be)

Triggers the Subjunctive directly (no να needed). - Ας πάμε! (Let's go!) - Ας είναι. (Let it be / Whatever).

Άντε / Άιντε (Come on!)

The most versatile word in Greek. It can mean "Let's go!", "Hurry up!", or "Get out of here!" - Άντε, σήκω! (Come on, get up!) - Άντε πάλι... (Oh, here we go again...)

4. Emotional Interjections

  • Μπράβο! (Well done!)
  • Α μπά; (Really? / No way!)
  • Αχ! (Oh! / Ah! - used for both pain and relief).
  • Αλίμονο! (Alas! / Of course! - depending on context).

🎯 Unit 22 Mastery Checklist

  • [ ] I can use Άραγε to express curiosity in a question.
  • [ ] I know that Μακάρι expresses a strong wish.
  • [ ] I understand the sarcastic tone of Δήθεν.
  • [ ] I know how to say "Let's go" using Ας πάμε.

Up Next: Unit 23: Relative Pronouns (που, ο οποίος)
